title: Kinematics
subject: Physics
content:
# Kinematics

## Quick Synopsis

Kinematics describes motion without asking why it happens. The core skill is choosing variables and axes so that displacement, velocity, and acceleration become simple relations.

## Must Know

- For constant acceleration, use equations of motion only along one axis at a time.
- Velocity is slope of x-t graph; acceleration is slope of v-t graph; displacement is area under v-t graph.
- Projectile motion separates into uniform horizontal motion and uniformly accelerated vertical motion.
- Relative velocity is velocity of one body measured from another: vAB = vA - vB.
- In circular motion, velocity is tangential and centripetal acceleration is toward the centre.
- Non-uniform circular motion has both radial and tangential acceleration.

## Formulas / Rules

- v = u + at; s = ut + 1/2 at²; v² = u² + 2as.
- Projectile range on level ground: R = u² sin2θ / g.
- Maximum height: H = u² sin²θ / 2g; time of flight: T = 2u sinθ / g.
- Centripetal acceleration: ar = v²/r = ω²r.
- Relative velocity: vrel = vobject - vobserver.

## JEE Focus

- For graphs, read slope and area before writing equations.
- In projectile questions, split initial velocity into u cosθ and u sinθ immediately.
- Use relative motion for rain-man, river-boat, collision approach, and moving platform questions.
- For constrained motion, differentiate length constraints carefully.

## Common Traps

- Distance and displacement are not the same.
- Average speed and magnitude of average velocity differ unless motion is straight without reversal.
- At the highest point of projectile, vertical velocity is zero but acceleration is still g downward.
- Centripetal acceleration changes direction even when speed is constant.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you solve one-dimensional motion from x-t and v-t graphs?
- Can you derive projectile range and height?
- Can you set up relative velocity in vector form?
- Can you handle circular motion acceleration components?
